threats.title: Assembly Evaluation Order Bug in onSetAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips Permanently Blocks Interest/Reserve Ratio Changes
threats.hypothesis_description: In HooksConfig.sol's onSetAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips, the Yul expression `or(lt(returndatasize(), 0x40), iszero(call(gas(), target, ...)))` evaluates arguments left-to-right per Yul specification. This means `returndatasize()` is evaluated BEFORE the `call()` opcode executes, reading the return data size from the PREVIOUS external call (typically `asset.balanceOf()` from `totalAssets()` in `_getUpdatedState()`, which returns 32 bytes). Since `lt(32, 0x40)` is always true, `or(true, X)` is always true, and the function always reverts regardless of the hook call's success. Any market deployed with `useOnSetAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips` enabled can never have its annual interest rate or reserve ratio changed.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a code-level bug in low-level assembly that permanently disables a critical borrower configuration function. Markets with parameter-constraining hooks (a core feature of the protocol) would be locked into their initial interest rate and reserve ratio settings forever.

proof_of_signal.pattern: or(lt(returndatasize(), 0x40), iszero(call(...))) — Yul evaluates lt(returndatasize(),...) BEFORE call(), using stale returndatasize from prior balanceOf (32 bytes). lt(32,64)=true makes or() always true, always reverting.

preconditions: Market deployed with useOnSetAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips flag enabled in HooksConfig
Borrower attempts to call setAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips
_getUpdatedState makes at least one external call returning <64 bytes (virtually always true: totalAssets→balanceOf returns 32 bytes)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: The only hook function with a two-value return (annualInterestBips, reserveRatioBips) uses a unique or() pattern not found in any other hook; all other hooks simply check iszero(call(...))
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Borrower is permanently locked out of parameter changes; no admin override exists
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent from market deployment onward — any market with this hook enabled is affected for its entire lifetime

operational_questions: How many existing markets have useOnSetAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips enabled?
Do the default hooks templates require this flag?
Is there any other code path to modify annualInterestBips or reserveRatioBips?
validation_requirements: Deploy market with hook flag enabled and attempt setAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips — expect revert
Verify Yul compilation order with EVM trace showing RETURNDATASIZE before CALL
Confirm no workaround exists to modify parameters via alternate path

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: No attacker action needed — the bug triggers for any borrower calling setAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips on an affected market
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Borrower discovers they cannot lower interest rates to reduce costs or adjust reserve ratio
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Market is stuck with initial parameters; borrower may be forced to overpay interest or maintain suboptimal reserve ratio
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Lenders may be trapped in unfavorable terms if market conditions change
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None — this is a latent code defect

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete loss of borrower parameter configuration capability for affected markets
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Borrower cannot respond to market conditions; lenders cannot benefit from parameter adjustments
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No on-chain recovery possible — markets are non-upgradeable; affected markets must be closed and new ones deployed
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: setAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips on all markets with the hook flag enabled

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: None required to trigger; Yul/EVM expertise required to understand root cause

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed transactions calling setAnnualInterestAndReserveRatioBips on hook-enabled markets
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Markets with stale interest rates despite changing conditions
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Borrowers deploying new markets to work around parameter lock

threats.title: closeMarket Permanently Blocked by Rounding-Induced Arithmetic Underflow in Withdrawal Batch Payment
threats.hypothesis_description: In closeMarket(), the expression `availableLiquidity -= normalizedAmountPaid` uses plain subtraction (not satSub). Due to asymmetric rounding between scaleAmount (rayDiv, rounds to nearest) and the reverse normalizeAmount via mulDiv (rounds down), normalizedAmountPaid can exceed availableLiquidity when scaleFactor >= 2*RAY. Example: scaleFactor=2e27, availableLiquidity=1 → scaleAmount(1)=1 → normalizedAmountPaid=mulDiv(1,2e27,1e27)=2 > 1 → underflow revert. Additionally, even without underflow, rounding dust in scaledPendingWithdrawals causes the final check `if (state.scaledPendingWithdrawals != 0) revert_CloseMarketWithUnpaidWithdrawals()` to trigger. This prevents the borrower from ever closing the market.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a mathematical precision error in a critical lifecycle function. Any market that accumulates 100%+ interest (scaleFactor >= 2*RAY) and has pending withdrawal batches with dust amounts becomes uncloseable. The borrower must continue paying interest indefinitely. repayAndProcessUnpaidWithdrawalBatches uses satSub for the same operation, confirming the inconsistency is unintentional.

proof_of_signal.pattern: availableLiquidity -= normalizedAmountPaid uses plain subtraction; compare with repayAndProcessUnpaidWithdrawalBatches which uses availableLiquidity.satSub(normalizedAmountPaid) at WildcatMarketWithdrawals.sol:186

preconditions: Market scaleFactor >= 2*RAY (100%+ cumulative interest accrued)
Market has pending or unpaid withdrawal batches with dust amounts
Borrower attempts to close the market after fully funding debts

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: closeMarket uses plain subtraction while repayAndProcessUnpaidWithdrawalBatches uses satSub for the identical operation — asymmetric safety handling
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Only borrower can call closeMarket; no alternative closure mechanism exists
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Becomes possible after sufficient interest accrual (months to years depending on APR); permanent once triggered

operational_questions: What is the minimum scaleFactor at which this triggers?
Can the borrower work around this by processing all unpaid batches first via repayAndProcessUnpaidWithdrawalBatches?
Does the scaledPendingWithdrawals dust check always fail even if the subtraction doesn't underflow?
validation_requirements: Create market with high APR, accrue interest until scaleFactor >= 2*RAY, queue withdrawal creating dust, attempt closeMarket
Verify arithmetic: scaleAmount(1) with scaleFactor=2e27 gives 1, mulDiv(1,2e27,1e27) gives 2
Test whether processing all batches externally before closeMarket avoids the issue

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for market to accumulate 100%+ interest (natural occurrence over time)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Queue a small withdrawal to create a batch with dust amounts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Borrower attempts closeMarket — transaction reverts with underflow or CloseMarketWithUnpaidWithdrawals
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Market cannot be closed; borrower continues paying interest indefinitely
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Any lender can create the dust condition by queuing a withdrawal; may occur naturally

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Borrower loses ability to terminate the market
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Borrower forced to pay interest indefinitely; market state cannot transition to closed
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No on-chain recovery — market is non-upgradeable
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: closeMarket for any market with sufficient interest accrual

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: Minimal market tokens needed to queue a dust withdrawal
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: After market has accrued 100%+ cumulative interest

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed closeMarket transactions, markets with very high scaleFactor
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Markets that remain open despite borrower attempts to close

threats.title: pushProtocolFeeBipsUpdates DoS When Any Market in Range Is Closed or Already Has Target Fee
threats.hypothesis_description: HooksFactory.pushProtocolFeeBipsUpdates iterates markets and calls market.setProtocolFeeBips on each. The setProtocolFeeBips function reverts if the market is closed (ProtocolFeeChangeOnClosedMarket) or if the fee hasn't changed (ProtocolFeeNotChanged). Since pushProtocolFeeBipsUpdates reverts on any individual market failure, a single closed or already-updated market blocks the entire batch. Anyone can call this function, and a borrower closing their market inadvertently or intentionally blocks fee updates for all other markets using the same hooks template.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is an architectural flaw where a batch operation has no error handling for individual failures. Protocol fee updates are important for revenue management, and blocking them affects all markets sharing a template. The function is permissionless, so anyone can trigger the failure.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Assembly call to market.setProtocolFeeBips reverts entire function on failure: `if iszero(call(...)) { mstore(0, 0x4484a4a9) revert(0x1c, 0x04) }` — no try/catch, no skip-on-failure

preconditions: At least one market in the iteration range is closed OR already has the target protocolFeeBips
Arch-controller owner has updated template fees via updateHooksTemplateFees

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Batch operation with no error tolerance — one bad market reverts the entire range update
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Arch-controller owner must manually paginate around problematic markets to update the rest
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time a market in the range is closed or already has the target fee

operational_questions: How many markets typically share a template?
Is there a practical workaround using start/end index parameters?
Could a borrower intentionally close a market to block fee updates on other markets?
validation_requirements: Deploy two markets, close one, attempt pushProtocolFeeBipsUpdates on both — expect revert
Verify that individual market.setProtocolFeeBips works when called directly by factory

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: pushProtocolFeeBipsUpdates called — reverts because closed market is in the range
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Other markets in the range don't receive fee updates
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None for natural occurrence; borrower can intentionally close to grief

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Fee updates blocked for entire market ranges until admin manually navigates around problematic markets
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low — admin can paginate using start/end indices, but requires manual intervention
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: pushProtocolFeeBipsUpdates for any template with closed markets

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: None — occurs naturally when any market closes
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: After any market in a template is closed

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Markets with stale protocol fee settings
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Admin repeatedly calling with different index ranges

threats.title: Withdrawal Batch Accumulation Gas Griefing Prevents closeMarket Execution
threats.hypothesis_description: A malicious lender can accumulate a large number of unpaid withdrawal batches by repeatedly queuing small withdrawals when the market is delinquent and the withdrawal batch duration is short (especially 0). Each batch that expires without full payment enters the unpaidBatches array. The closeMarket function has an unbounded loop iterating all unpaid batches, potentially exceeding the block gas limit. Additionally, the loop always calls _processUnpaidWithdrawalBatch which reads first() from unpaidBatches — if the first batch isn't fully paid, the loop processes the same batch repeatedly for numBatches iterations, wasting gas without making progress on other batches.
threats.investigation_rationale: closeMarket's loop over unpaidBatches is unbounded and cannot be incrementally processed like repayAndProcessUnpaidWithdrawalBatches. A borrower would need to clear the queue via repayAndProcessUnpaidWithdrawalBatches before calling closeMarket, adding operational complexity and cost.
deployment_assumptions: Market deployed with short withdrawalBatchDuration (0 or near 0)
Market becomes delinquent for sustained period
threats.config_evidence: src/market/WildcatMarketBase.sol constructor: withdrawalBatchDuration set from parameters; short duration enables rapid batch creation

proof_of_signal.pattern: Unbounded loop: `for (uint256 i; i < numBatches; i++) { ... _processUnpaidWithdrawalBatch ... }` where numBatches = _withdrawalData.unpaidBatches.length() with no gas check or max limit

preconditions: Market has short withdrawalBatchDuration (ideally 0)
Market is delinquent (insufficient reserves to fully pay batches)
Attacker has market tokens to queue withdrawals
Attacker queues tiny withdrawals over many blocks to accumulate unpaid batches

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: closeMarket iterates ALL unpaid batches unboundedly; _processUnpaidWithdrawalBatch always reads first() so unpaid first batch causes repeated processing
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Borrower must clear unpaid batches externally before calling closeMarket
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Market must be delinquent with short batch duration for attacker to accumulate batches

operational_questions: What is the gas cost per batch iteration in closeMarket?
How many batches can accumulate before exceeding block gas limit?
Can repayAndProcessUnpaidWithdrawalBatches fully clear the queue as a workaround?
validation_requirements: Deploy market with withdrawalBatchDuration=0 and delinquent state
Queue many small withdrawals across blocks to accumulate unpaid batches
Attempt closeMarket and measure gas consumption

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deposit into market to obtain market tokens
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait for or cause market delinquency (borrower extracts reserves up to limit)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Queue tiny withdrawals (1 wei) across many blocks, each creating a new unpaid batch
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: After accumulating 1000+ batches, closeMarket exceeds block gas limit
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Single attacker with market tokens; requires patience (many blocks)

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Borrower forced to pay interest indefinitely; must use workaround of clearing batches first
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium — borrower can use repayAndProcessUnpaidWithdrawalBatches to incrementally clear batches, but this adds cost and complexity
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: closeMarket for markets with many accumulated unpaid batches

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: Minimal market tokens (1 wei per withdrawal)
execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low — single attacker, but requires many blocks
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: During market delinquency; requires many blocks to accumulate batches

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Many small withdrawal queue transactions from same address; large unpaidBatches array
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Markets with unusually many unpaid batch entries

threats.title: onBorrow Hook Assembly Uses Wrong Size Constant (RepayHook_Base_Size Instead of BorrowHook_Base_Size)
threats.hypothesis_description: In HooksConfig.sol's onBorrow function, the assembly block calculates the hook call size using `add(RepayHook_Base_Size, extraCalldataBytes)` instead of `add(BorrowHook_Base_Size, extraCalldataBytes)`. Both constants are currently defined as 0x0224, so the bug has no immediate impact. However, if the borrow and repay hook parameter structures diverge in a future version or fork, this would produce incorrect calldata sizing for the borrow hook, potentially truncating or padding the hook call incorrectly.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a confirmed code defect where the wrong named constant is used. While currently harmless due to identical values, it indicates insufficient review of the assembly code and could cause silent corruption if parameters change.

proof_of_signal.pattern: let size := add(RepayHook_Base_Size, extraCalldataBytes) — should be BorrowHook_Base_Size; both happen to equal 0x0224

preconditions: Market with useOnBorrow hook flag enabled
BorrowHook_Base_Size and RepayHook_Base_Size must diverge for actual impact (not currently the case)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Wrong constant name used in assembly — semantic mismatch between function purpose (borrow) and constant used (repay)
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: None currently; would become exploitable if hook parameter structures change

operational_questions: Are there plans to change borrow hook parameters that would make them differ from repay?
Should this be classified as a maintenance risk or an active vulnerability?
validation_requirements: Verify BorrowHook_Base_Size == RepayHook_Base_Size == 0x0224
Confirm hook call works correctly despite wrong constant name

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Would become exploitable if borrow/repay hook parameter structures diverge
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Incorrect calldata size could cause hook to read garbage data or truncate valid data
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Hook may silently process incorrect parameters, potentially allowing unauthorized borrows

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: None currently; potential future risk if parameters change
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires new market deployment to fix

threats.title: withdrawableProtocolFees Arithmetic Underflow Causes collectFees Denial of Service During Extreme Delinquency
threats.hypothesis_description: MarketState.withdrawableProtocolFees computes `uint256 totalAvailableAssets = totalAssets - state.normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals` without safe subtraction. If the market is severely delinquent and totalAssets < normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals (e.g., borrower has withdrawn most funds), this subtraction causes an arithmetic underflow panic (revert). This prevents collectFees from executing, blocking protocol fee collection. While the primary impact is on the fee recipient, it also affects any integrations that depend on collectFees being callable.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is an unchecked subtraction in a function called by collectFees. During severe delinquency, the protocol cannot collect fees. While delinquency is an abnormal state, the code should handle it gracefully rather than panic.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 totalAvailableAssets = totalAssets - state.normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals — unchecked subtraction that panics when totalAssets < normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals

preconditions: Market is severely delinquent: borrower has withdrawn assets below normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals threshold
accruedProtocolFees > 0
Fee recipient or anyone calls collectFees

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Plain subtraction without satSub in a function that can naturally encounter the underflow condition during delinquency
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Fee recipient cannot collect fees during severe delinquency
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During any period where totalAssets < normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals

operational_questions: How likely is it for totalAssets to drop below normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals in practice?
Does the external view function withdrawableProtocolFees() also panic?
Does this block any other critical functionality?
validation_requirements: Create market, accrue fees, borrow until totalAssets < normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals, call collectFees
Verify panic revert instead of graceful zero return

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Borrower borrows maximum allowed amount, creating delinquency
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Withdrawal batches are processed, increasing normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires borrower to be delinquent — not directly attacker-controlled

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Fee recipient cannot collect accrued fees until market returns to solvency
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Self-resolving once borrower repays and totalAssets > normalizedUnclaimedWithdrawals

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Inability to withdraw fees during delinquent periods

threats.title: Deposit Transfer Event Emits Input Amount Instead of Actual Minted Normalized Value
threats.hypothesis_description: In _depositUpTo, the ERC-20 Transfer event is emitted as `emit_Transfer(address(0), msg.sender, amount)` where `amount` is the user-supplied deposit amount (capped by maximumDeposit). However, the actual balance change is `normalizeAmount(scaledAmount)` = `normalizeAmount(scaleAmount(amount))` which can differ from `amount` due to rounding in the scale/normalize round-trip. This means the Transfer event reports a different value than the actual balance change, violating ERC-20 semantics. External integrations (block explorers, portfolio trackers, DEX aggregators) that rely on Transfer events for accounting will have discrepancies.
threats.investigation_rationale: ERC-20 Transfer events are universally used for off-chain tracking. Incorrect event amounts cause accounting discrepancies in indexers, wallets, and DeFi protocols that track token balances via events rather than direct queries.

proof_of_signal.pattern: emit_Transfer(_runtimeConstant(address(0)), msg.sender, amount) — emits input `amount` not `normalizeAmount(scaledAmount)` which is the actual balance change

preconditions: Any deposit where scaleAmount followed by normalizeAmount produces a different value (extremely common due to integer rounding)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Transfer event amount disagrees with actual balanceOf change by up to 1 wei
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None — affects all deposits and transfers

operational_questions: Do any DeFi integrations depend on Transfer event amounts for accounting?
What is the maximum discrepancy in practice (typically 1 wei)?
Does this affect _transfer in WildcatMarketToken similarly?
validation_requirements: Deposit an amount where normalizeAmount(scaleAmount(amount)) != amount
Compare Transfer event amount with balanceOf delta

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deposit any amount to the market
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Compare Transfer event amount with actual balanceOf change
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Note discrepancy of up to 1 wei per operation
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Accumulates across many operations for off-chain accounting systems

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Transfer event amounts differ from balanceOf deltas
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Off-chain balance trackers showing slight discrepancies
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Consistent 1-wei discrepancies in event vs balance tracking

threats.title: createEscrow Unrestricted Caller Can Grief tmpEscrowParams in Concurrent Transaction Contexts
threats.hypothesis_description: WildcatSanctionsSentinel.createEscrow is public with no access control or reentrancy guard. It modifies tmpEscrowParams (storage slot) before deploying the escrow contract, then resets it. If a market's executeWithdrawal calls _createEscrowForUnderlyingAsset → sentinel.createEscrow in the middle of a transaction, and the asset token has a callback (e.g., ERC-777 or similar), the callback could call createEscrow with different parameters, corrupting tmpEscrowParams. The WildcatSanctionsEscrow constructor reads tmpEscrowParams to set its immutable borrower/account/asset, potentially reading attacker-supplied values. However, the CREATE2 salt is derived from the CORRECT parameters, so the deployment address is still deterministic per the correct triple — a deployment with wrong params would go to a different address, and the correct-address deployment would fail.
threats.investigation_rationale: While the CREATE2 salt mitigates the worst case (wrong escrow params at the correct address), the lack of access control on createEscrow combined with mutable storage params is a risky pattern. On chains where callback tokens exist, this warrants verification that the mitigation (address mismatch) holds in all edge cases.
deployment_assumptions: Underlying asset token has transfer callbacks (ERC-777 or similar)
Multiple escrow creations possible within one transaction
threats.config_evidence: src/WildcatSanctionsSentinel.sol createEscrow function: no access control modifier, no reentrancy guard; tmpEscrowParams stored in regular storage (not transient)

proof_of_signal.pattern: tmpEscrowParams = TmpEscrowParams(borrower, account, asset) written to storage before CREATE2; no reentrancy guard; constructor reads tmpEscrowParams via staticcall

preconditions: Underlying asset supports transfer callbacks (ERC-777 or hook-enabled token)
Sanctioned account triggers escrow creation path during executeWithdrawal
Attacker contract receives callback during asset transfer and calls createEscrow with different params

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: tmpEscrowParams is mutable storage shared across all callers with no guard; pattern similar to known reentrancy through callback tokens
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No access control on createEscrow — anyone can call it
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During asset transfer in executeWithdrawal if token has callbacks

operational_questions: Does the CREATE2 salt derivation fully prevent wrong params at correct address?
Can callback-equipped tokens be used as market underlying assets?
Does the market's nonReentrant guard prevent re-entry into createEscrow indirectly?
validation_requirements: Deploy market with ERC-777 token, trigger sanctioned withdrawal, verify escrow params
Test reentrancy into createEscrow during asset transfer callback
Verify CREATE2 address mismatch when wrong params are supplied

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Get sanctioned account to have balance in the market
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Callback calls sentinel.createEscrow with attacker-chosen params, corrupting tmpEscrowParams
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires callback-enabled underlying token — blocked by project's token constraints

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Potential escrow misconfiguration if exploitation succeeds
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Sanctioned user's funds could be sent to wrong escrow
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Funds at wrong escrow address would be recoverable if escrow params allow release
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: executeWithdrawal for sanctioned accounts with callback-enabled tokens

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: None directly; needs market with callback token
execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Medium — requires callback token and sanctioned account
execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Advanced — exploit requires precise callback timing

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Unexpected createEscrow calls during market operations

threats.title: closeMarket Loop Repeatedly Processes Same First Unpaid Batch Without Progressing Queue
threats.hypothesis_description: In closeMarket's unpaid batch processing loop, _processUnpaidWithdrawalBatch always reads the first batch from the unpaidBatches queue via `_withdrawalData.unpaidBatches.first()`. If this first batch is not fully paid after processing, it is NOT removed from the queue (removal only occurs when `batch.scaledTotalAmount == batch.scaledAmountBurned`). The loop iterates `numBatches` times but processes the same batch every iteration with decreasing availableLiquidity. This means: (1) if the first batch can't be fully paid, no subsequent batch gets any payment, and (2) the subtraction `availableLiquidity -= normalizedAmountPaid` accumulates and may underflow. Combined with the rounding issue in OPS-H002, this amplifies the probability of closeMarket failure.
threats.investigation_rationale: The loop logic assumes each iteration processes a DIFFERENT batch, but the implementation always reads the first unpaid batch. This design flaw wastes gas and compounds the rounding underflow risk with each iteration.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _processUnpaidWithdrawalBatch always calls `_withdrawalData.unpaidBatches.first()` regardless of loop iteration; batch only removed on full payment via shift()

preconditions: Market has multiple unpaid withdrawal batches
First batch requires more liquidity than available to be fully paid
Borrower attempts to close market

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Loop iterates numBatches times but processes same first batch each time; contrast with repayAndProcessUnpaidWithdrawalBatches which has `availableLiquidity > 0` guard
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Borrower must clear unpaid batches via repayAndProcessUnpaidWithdrawalBatches before closeMarket
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time market has multiple unpaid batches with insufficient liquidity for first batch

operational_questions: Does the repeated processing converge to fully paying the first batch?
What happens when normalizedAmountPaid rounds to 0 on subsequent iterations?
Is there an edge case where the subtraction underflows on iteration > 1?
validation_requirements: Create market with multiple unpaid batches, fund partially, attempt closeMarket
Trace gas consumption across loop iterations
Verify whether availableLiquidity underflows on non-first iteration

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: As any lender, create multiple withdrawal batches while market is delinquent
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Borrower funds market to close it but first batch requires all liquidity
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Loop processes first batch, then tries same batch again with remaining dust
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Rounding causes normalizedAmountPaid > remaining availableLiquidity on subsequent iteration — underflow revert
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Single lender can create multiple unpaid batches over time

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: closeMarket blocked when multiple unpaid batches exist
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Borrower must pre-process all batches via repayAndProcessUnpaidWithdrawalBatches
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium — workaround exists but adds operational burden
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: closeMarket with multiple unpaid withdrawal batches

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: Market tokens to create withdrawal batches
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: When market has accumulated unpaid batches

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: closeMarket transactions failing with arithmetic errors
